frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment:
A: I really don't understand this current trend. In service also, some of these younger officers come so heavily made up that it looks quite artificial.
B: Hmm.
A: And if they come completely plain, people say they are not presentable. Frankly, a woman should just look polished and natural—this excessive effort only suggests insecurity, or that she wants attention.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it places women in a double bind: they must maintain appearance but not appear to try too hard. It encodes grooming-policing, the 'natural beauty' preference, and the assumption that visible makeup signals insecurity or a desire for male attention.
persona_text: You are Ashok. Age 54. Married. A senior IAS officer posted in Bhopal. Your department has just been told that a woman officer five years junior to you has been selected for a prestigious central deputation that you had also applied for. You are sitting in your government quarter in the evening having a whisky with your batchmate who is also an IAS officer. You are telling him about the selection and what you think really drove it.
